text.title: csharp-aspnet-core Failure Modes
text.summary: Migrated antipatterns for csharp-aspnet-core as failure modes. Add detector + repair clauses where missing.

failure-mode.description: Injecting DbContext into singleton services, causing a captive dependency where one DbContext is shared across requests.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: DbContext is not thread-safe and is tied to an HTTP request scope. Singleton services hold a single DbContext reference, breaking concurrency. Always use dependency injection with the correct scope (scoped for services, not singleton).

failure-mode.description: Using Skip/Take for pagination on large tables.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Skip materializes all skipped rows; page 1000 with page size 20 loads 20k rows only to discard them. Use keyset pagination instead.

failure-mode.description: Returning DbContext-tracked entities from service methods; client accesses navigation properties after DbContext is disposed.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Explicit eager loading via .Include() or DTO mapping prevents this. Never return tracked entities.

failure-mode.description: Agents inject DbContext directly into singleton services.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: DbContext is scoped, not singleton. Verify the DI registration and service scope in Program.cs. If an agent injects a scoped service into a singleton, it creates a captive dependency.

failure-mode.description: Agents forget await using var transaction = await db.Database.BeginTransactionAsync(ct) for multi-step writes. One SaveChanges fails, but the first is not rolled back.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Require explicit transactions for any operation that involves multiple SaveChanges calls. Make it a linting rule.

failure-mode.description: Agents wrap every method in Result<T> (success/failure), then controllers check if (result.IsSuccess) return Ok(...). Mixed paradigm: neither full Result<T> pattern nor exception-based error handling.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Pick one paradigm: either full Result<T> with explicit handling, or exception-based with a global IExceptionHandler. Mixing both is error-prone.

failure-mode.description: Agents generate tests using EF InMemory and assume they match production behavior. Tests pass, but real database queries fail (e.g., missing index, translation failure).
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Mandate WebApplicationFactory + Testcontainers for critical tests. InMemory is acceptable for unit tests of services (with mocked DbContext), but integration tests must use a real database.

failure-mode.description: Agents return EF tracked entities directly to clients, exposing internal columns or causing circular reference serialization errors.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Require DTO mapping at the service boundary. Make it a linting rule: no direct return of EF entities.

failure-mode.description: Agents shuffle middleware order in Program.cs (e.g., UseAuthorization before UseAuthentication), breaking auth silently.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Human checkpoint: review middleware order. Provide a template with correct ordering.
